headline.resting: Lean back and rest a moment
headline.bad: Take a moment — straighten your shoulders

subline.paused: Resume to start checking your posture again
subline.away: We'll resume automatically when you return
subline.resting: We'll resume automatically when you sit up straight
subline.standing: Standing straightens your spine. Try raising both arms for a light stretch.

aiGuide.none: No posture issues detected today yet! You're maintaining excellent posture. Periodic light stretching is all you need.
aiGuide.forwardHead: Forward head and sitting too close to the screen are your main issues. Raise your monitor 5–10 cm so your eye line meets the top third of the screen. This helps reduce tension in the neck and shoulders.
aiGuide.slouching: Slouching was detected often. Press your pelvis fully against the chair back and keep your knees at 90°. Supporting your lumbar curve helps reduce load on the lower back.
aiGuide.chinResting: Chin resting was observed somewhat. It tends to concentrate force on one side and can throw off shoulder balance. Rest both elbows lightly on the desk to spread the load more evenly.
aiGuide.generic: {{label}} was detected most often. After 50 minutes of focus, always loosen your shoulders for 1 minute and add light full-body stretching to relieve joint pressure.

health.label: Posture health score
health.tooltip: Today's posture health score and grade, calculated from real-time detection data. (S/A/B/C/D grading)
health.ground: Linked to the Nachemson spinal mechanics model and the American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons (AAOS) alignment criteria.

violations.label: Posture violations
violations.tooltip: How often posture violations such as forward head, slouching, and chin resting were detected during today's real-time monitoring.
violations.ground: Holding one position for a long time tends to build up static muscle fatigue. Rather than keeping a single 'correct' posture, changing position and moving often matters more.

stretch.label: Stretch score
stretch.tooltip: Today's score for resetting static spinal load with chest openers, shoulder stretches, and more.
stretch.ground: The longer you sit, the more it helps to get up and move periodically. About every 50 minutes, change posture with roughly a minute of stretching or walking to ease muscle tension. (per KOSHA guidance)

usage.label: Total time today
usage.tooltip: Total active work time during which the real-time posture analysis engine ran to precisely protect your spinal health.
usage.ground: Reminders such as on-screen prompts are reported to help reduce sitting time and increase movement (meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials). Small but consistent nudges build posture habits.

D.label: At risk
D.desc: A pattern that can lead to fatigue or discomfort if it accumulates over time

camera.helpBody1: When your camera angle changes (laptop/monitor, etc.), body recognition can distort geometrically. We recommend setting a new baseline to prevent misdetection.
camera.helpBody2: 💡 Register each angle (front/left/right) just once, and afterward it auto-recognizes in real time even if the camera moves — no extra setup needed!

report.timeTrend: Sitting time & violation trend by hour
report.perfTitle: 📈 Your posture-training results in numbers (vs yesterday)
report.gradeKeep: Today's grade: {{grade}} ({{label}}). Straighten your back and aim for grade A or higher.
report.reactionFast: You react very quickly when you notice bad posture!
report.reactionSlow: Correct your posture right when you get an alert.
report.goodTimeKeep: Try to increase your posture-holding time vs yesterday.
report.basisTitle: Scientific spinal-mechanics basis for the posture health grade
report.basisBody: The grade classification (S/A/B/C/D) in this report is designed with reference to Nachemson's spinal biomechanics research on intradiscal pressure and the NASA Neutral Body Posture guideline as biomechanical models. These models estimate that seated disc load tends to be higher than standing, and that load on the neck grows as the head moves forward — note that such figures are model-based estimates. That said, there is no single 'perfect posture': not holding one position for too long and changing it often matters more. Use this metric as a reference to help build the habit of resetting your posture frequently.
report.trendTitle: Posture health score trend by hour (today)
report.legendTitle: Grade legend (linked to Nachemson spinal-mechanics weighting):
report.legendD: D (static load building up · move often recommended)
report.proGift1: • Personalized clinical advice based on real-time detection data
report.proGift2: • Daily/weekly/monthly trend reports of neck angle and shoulder balance
report.proGift4: • Desktop app integration (native background + widget included)

clinicalWarn.forward_head: This position tends to concentrate static load on the neck. Try gently pulling your chin back and changing posture often.
clinicalWarn.chin_resting: Resting on one hand can put uneven tension on the jaw and neck. Rest both elbows lightly and switch your position regularly.
clinicalWarn.shoulder_tilt: Leaning to one side puts biased load on the shoulders. Try to balance both sides and reset your posture often.
clinicalWarn.slouching: Slumping tends to increase load on the lower back. Sit your pelvis back into the chair and stand up to move now and then.
clinicalWarn.monitor_too_close: Sitting too close tends to tire your eyes and tense your neck. Step back a little and let your eyes rest periodically.
clinicalWarn.shoulder_asymmetry: Uneven shoulders concentrate tension on one side. Loosen both shoulders and change posture often.
clinicalWarn.head_roll: Tilting your head to one side puts tension on one side of the neck. Keep it level and move often.

biomechDetail.forward_head: As the head moves forward, load on the neck joints tends to increase. Pull your chin back gently and change position often.
biomechDetail.slouching: Slumped alignment tends to raise load on the lower back. Support your lumbar curve and reset your posture often.
biomechDetail.shoulder_tilt: Dropping one shoulder tends to build up tension on that side. Try to balance both shoulders and move often.
biomechDetail.chin_resting: Resting your chin on your hand puts uneven force on the jaw. Spread the load with both elbows and switch position often.
biomechDetail.monitor_too_close: Sitting close tends to keep your eye muscles tense. Step back a little and rest your eyes periodically.
biomechDetail.shoulder_asymmetry: Uneven shoulders tend to concentrate load on one side. Balance both sides and change posture often.
biomechDetail.head_roll: Tilting your head puts tension on one side of the neck. Keep it level and move often.
biomechDetail.default: Adjust your chair and monitor heights to a comfortable position — and above all, avoid holding one posture too long. Changing position often matters most.

report.noViolationOnDate: 👏 No {{label}} violations were recorded on the selected date!
report.proOnlyTitle: The precision analysis report is a PRO feature
report.proOnlyDesc: Based on today's accumulated data, **clinical physical-therapy and ergonomic AI advice feedback** and **second-level precision timestamp logs** are advanced features available only to PRO members.
report.proOnlyDesc_beta: Check out the ergonomic AI feedback advice based on today's accumulated data and the second-level precision timestamp log. You can check the upcoming roadmap for advanced stats and AI coaching in the community.
